title: Capacity for movement is an organisational principle in object representations: EEG data from Experiment 2
description: This dataset contains electroencephalographic (EEG) recordings from 24 participants performing object classification and passive viewing tasks while viewing static images of objects with varying capacities for movement and aliveness. The study investigates how the human brain represents movement as an organizational principle in visual object processing, independent of animacy. Raw EEG data in BrainVision format, preprocessed derivatives in EEGlab and cosmoMVPA formats, experimental stimuli (400 CC0 images), analysis scripts, and decoding results are provided.
methods_description: Participants completed two tasks: (1) a time-pressured classification task where participants classified single images as 'can move' or 'still' with a 1-second timeout, and (2) a passive viewing task with rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP) streams of images where participants indicated fixation cross color changes. EEG data were recorded during both tasks.
